title: Stimulated emission: Einstein's missing rate, and why every laser is a 1917 thought experiment
intro: Around 1916 Einstein had spent a decade circling Planck's blackbody formula and decided to re-derive it from the ground up, replacing the cavity oscillators Planck had counted with atomic absorption and emission rates. The bookkeeping did not balance. To close the books, Einstein had to postulate a third rate that no one had yet measured and no one had named, by which a photon already present could induce an excited atom to emit a second photon identical to itself. He called it induzierte Emission. Forty-three years later, in a basement at Hughes Research Laboratories in Malibu, Theodore Maiman built the first device in which Einstein's third rate, summed over a centimeter-long rod of chromium ions, produced a half-millisecond pulse of red light coherent enough to call a laser.

sections.heading: 1. Einstein, 1916: the books do not close

blocks.text: By 1916 Einstein had spent a decade circling Planck's 1900 spectral energy density of a blackbody, ρ(ν, T), and the derivation troubled him. Planck had counted electromagnetic modes in a closed cavity, assigned each one a discrete energy of the form E = nhν (where n is a non-negative integer, ν the mode frequency, and h the quantum of action introduced for the purpose), and summed. The trick produced the right curve, in the sense that ρ(ν, T) matched every experiment from the long-wavelength Rayleigh-Jeans regime to the short-wavelength Wien tail. It also pointed nowhere. The cavity oscillators were a mathematical bookkeeping device with no obvious connection to the atoms in the cavity walls that actually emitted and absorbed the light. Einstein wanted a derivation built from the atomic side.

He set up the simplest possible atom: two non-degenerate levels, a ground state |1⟩ and an excited state |2⟩, separated by an energy gap hν₀. He assumed two rates were known. The first was absorption: an atom in |1⟩ could absorb a photon and jump to |2⟩ at a per-atom rate B₁₂ ρ(ν₀), where B₁₂ was an unknown coefficient and ρ(ν₀) the spectral energy density at the transition frequency. The second was spontaneous emission: an atom in |2⟩ could fall to |1⟩ on its own at a fixed per-atom rate A₂₁, independent of the surrounding light. At thermal equilibrium, the populations N₁ (number of atoms in |1⟩ per unit volume) and N₂ (number of atoms in |2⟩ per unit volume) must follow the Boltzmann ratio for two non-degenerate states of an ensemble at temperature T:

blocks.caption: N₁ is the number density of atoms in the ground state |1⟩. N₂ is the number density of atoms in the excited state |2⟩. h is Planck's constant, 6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s. ν₀ is the frequency of the |1⟩ to |2⟩ transition, so that hν₀ is the energy gap between the two levels. k_B is Boltzmann's constant, 1.381 × 10⁻²³ J/K. T is the absolute temperature of the gas of atoms and radiation in thermal equilibrium. The right-hand side is the canonical Boltzmann factor of statistical mechanics for the relative occupation of two non-degenerate states.

blocks.text: Einstein then demanded that absorption and emission balance in equilibrium: the rate at which atoms leave |1⟩ must equal the rate at which they leave |2⟩. With only absorption on the upward side and only spontaneous emission on the downward side, the balance reads N₁ B₁₂ ρ(ν₀) = N₂ A₂₁. Solving for ρ(ν₀) gives ρ = (A₂₁/B₁₂)(N₂/N₁), and substituting the Boltzmann ratio for N₂/N₁ gives ρ(ν₀) = (A₂₁/B₁₂) exp(−hν₀/k_BT). This is the wrong formula. Planck's law has a 1/[exp(hν₀/k_BT) − 1] in it, not exp(−hν₀/k_BT). At very high frequencies the two coincide (both go to zero), and Einstein could recover the Wien tail. At low frequencies they diverge: Planck's law approaches the Rayleigh-Jeans 8πν²k_BT/c³, while Einstein's two-rate model predicts a finite constant. The books did not close. Something was missing on the emission side of the ledger.

sections.heading: 2. A third rate, and a photon that breeds a clone

blocks.text: Einstein closed the books by adding a third rate. An atom already in the upper state |2⟩, struck by a photon of energy hν₀ from the surrounding field, could be tickled into emitting a second photon and dropping back to |1⟩. The new photon would not be a random emission: it would match the inducing photon in frequency, in direction, in phase, and in polarization. He wrote the rate as B₂₁ ρ(ν₀), with B₂₁ a new coefficient on the emission side. The corrected detailed balance, with absorption equal to the sum of spontaneous and stimulated emission, reads:

blocks.caption: N₁ is the number density of atoms in the lower state |1⟩. B₁₂ is the absorption rate coefficient, the constant of proportionality between the spectral energy density and the per-atom transition rate from |1⟩ to |2⟩. ρ(ν₀) is the spectral energy density of the radiation field at the transition frequency ν₀, in units of energy per unit volume per unit frequency. N₂ is the number density of atoms in the upper state |2⟩. A₂₁ is the spontaneous-emission rate, the per-atom probability per unit time of decay from |2⟩ to |1⟩ in the absence of any external field. B₂₁ is the stimulated-emission rate coefficient, the constant Einstein introduced to balance the books, fixing the per-atom rate of induced emission from |2⟩ to |1⟩ as proportional to ρ(ν₀).

blocks.text: Solve this equation for ρ(ν₀): ρ = N₂ A₂₁ / (N₁ B₁₂ − N₂ B₂₁). Divide top and bottom by N₂ to get ρ = A₂₁ / [(N₁/N₂) B₁₂ − B₂₁], then substitute the Boltzmann ratio N₁/N₂ = exp(+hν₀/k_BT), and the result is ρ(ν₀) = A₂₁ / [B₁₂ exp(hν₀/k_BT) − B₂₁]. Compare this term-by-term with the Planck spectral density, ρ(ν₀, T) = (8πhν₀³/c³) / [exp(hν₀/k_BT) − 1]. The two agree if and only if the bracket in the denominator matches: that forces B₁₂ = B₂₁, and the prefactor then forces A₂₁/B₂₁ to take a specific value. The Einstein relations between the three coefficients fall out:

blocks.caption: B₁₂ is the absorption rate coefficient. B₂₁ is the stimulated-emission rate coefficient. The first equation states that they are equal: the per-atom rates of absorption and of stimulated emission, at the same radiation density and the same transition frequency, are identical. A₂₁ is the spontaneous-emission rate. The second equation gives the ratio of spontaneous to stimulated rate coefficients. h is Planck's constant. ν₀ is the transition frequency. c is the speed of light in vacuum, 2.998 × 10⁸ m/s. The factor 8πν₀²/c³ is the number of electromagnetic modes per unit volume per unit frequency in three dimensions, the same density of states Planck used in his 1900 derivation, and the extra hν₀ converts a per-mode quantity to a per-photon energy quantity.

blocks.text: Two things fall out for free. First, at thermal equilibrium with hν₀ much greater than k_BT (visible light at room temperature, where hν₀/k_BT ≈ 80 and the Boltzmann factor is roughly 10⁻³⁵), spontaneous emission completely dominates stimulated emission, the upper state is essentially empty, and the gas absorbs much more light than it emits in stimulated mode. The light you see from a hot filament is essentially all spontaneous. Second, and this is the part with consequences, the stimulated photon is a clone. It shares phase, frequency, direction, and polarization with the inducing photon (Paul Dirac proved this rigorously in 1927 from the quantum theory of the electromagnetic field, by noticing that the matrix element for emission into a mode already containing n photons is enhanced by a factor √(n+1) compared with emission into an empty mode, of which n comes from stimulated emission and the leftover 1 from spontaneous). One photon of the right energy can leave an excited atom carrying two photons that march in step. That is the entire physical content of laser light.

quiz.question: Consider visible photons at frequency ν₀ = 5 × 10¹⁴ Hz (the green part of the spectrum) inside the cavity of a tungsten-filament incandescent bulb at T = 3000 K. Using the Einstein relations, what is the approximate ratio of stimulated-emission rate to spontaneous-emission rate, per atom, integrated over the blackbody radiation present in the cavity?
options: About 1: at any temperature above absolute zero, stimulated and spontaneous emission contribute roughly equally to the total photon output.

About 3 × 10⁻⁴. The ratio of stimulated to spontaneous rates is the photon occupation number n̄(ν₀, T) = 1/[exp(hν₀/k_BT) − 1] of the blackbody mode at the transition frequency. With h = 6.6 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s, ν₀ = 5 × 10¹⁴ Hz, k_B = 1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K, and T = 3000 K, the exponent hν₀/k_BT ≈ 8, so 1/[e⁸ − 1] ≈ 3 × 10⁻⁴.
About 10⁻³⁵, the same as the Boltzmann factor between the lower and upper atomic states.
About 10³: the stimulated-emission cross section is famously much larger than the spontaneous one in the visible band.
quiz.explanation: Option 0 ignores the temperature dependence of the photon occupation number; equality is only approached when k_BT exceeds hν₀, which fails by a factor of ten for visible light at 3000 K. Option 2 confuses the upper-to-lower atomic population ratio with the stimulated-to-spontaneous rate ratio. Those are different quantities: the upper-state population is suppressed by exp(−hν₀/k_BT), but the per-atom stimulated rate divided by the per-atom spontaneous rate is the per-mode photon occupation, n̄ = 1/[exp(hν₀/k_BT) − 1]. Option 3 has the magnitudes backwards. The correct answer is option 1. Use the Einstein relation A₂₁/B₂₁ = 8πhν₀³/c³ and the Planck formula ρ(ν₀, T) = (8πhν₀³/c³)/[exp(hν₀/k_BT) − 1]. The per-atom stimulated rate is B₂₁ ρ(ν₀, T), the per-atom spontaneous rate is A₂₁, and the ratio is exactly ρ(ν₀,T) B₂₁ / A₂₁ = 1/[exp(hν₀/k_BT) − 1]. At 3000 K and green-light frequency, the exponent is about 8, and the ratio comes out near 3 × 10⁻⁴. Stimulated emission is genuinely happening inside a tungsten filament, but per atom it lags spontaneous emission by a factor of three thousand. To flip the ratio you either flood the cavity with photons (high mode occupation, the laser case) or you push the populations the wrong way around (inversion, N₂ > N₁); both routes turn out to require the same engineering, which is what section 3 takes up.

sections.heading: 3. Inversion, and why no flashlight ever lased

blocks.text: The detailed balance of section 2 hides a useful inequality. Define the net stimulated rate as the difference between the rate at which photons are added to the field by stimulated emission and the rate at which they are removed by absorption. With B₁₂ = B₂₁ ≡ B from the Einstein relation, this net rate per unit volume is (N₂ − N₁) B ρ(ν₀). The sign of N₂ − N₁ decides everything. When N₂ < N₁, as in any thermal gas at any positive temperature, the bracket is negative and a beam passing through the medium is attenuated. When N₂ > N₁, the bracket flips sign and the beam is amplified. The crossover, N₂ = N₁, is called transparency, and the regime N₂ > N₁ is called population inversion. Every laser exists because someone has engineered inversion in a piece of matter; every non-laser exists because nature, left alone, refuses to deliver it.

Inversion cannot be reached by shining light at a two-level atom. The argument is short. Every photon absorbed at frequency ν₀ lifts a |1⟩ atom to |2⟩ at rate B ρ N₁; every photon stimulated downward by the same field returns a |2⟩ atom to |1⟩ at rate B ρ N₂; the two rates are equal because B₁₂ = B₂₁. At very low pump intensity, N₂ stays near zero and absorption dominates. At very high pump intensity, the populations equalize at N₁ = N₂ and the net rate saturates at zero. The medium has become transparent. The medium has not become an amplifier. Optical pumping of a two-level transition produces saturation, never gain. That was the conceptual barrier the Bell Labs and Hughes laser groups all had to climb around between 1958 and 1960.

blocks.caption: Steady-state populations of two model laser media under pump rate R relative to the spontaneous-emission rate γ of the lasing transition. Left: a three-level scheme after Maiman's 1960 ruby, with the ground state as the lower laser level. Inversion (Δ = N_upper − N_lower greater than zero) appears only above R = γ, the point at which more than half the active ions have been pumped up. Right: a four-level scheme after Geusic's 1964 Nd:YAG, with a short-lived lower laser level that drains to ground at a fast rate Γ much larger than γ. The lower laser level is always essentially empty, and inversion is reached at any R greater than zero. Drag the slider through five decades of pump rate and watch the three-level scheme fight for inversion while the four-level scheme has it for free.

blocks.text: The way around the barrier is a third energy level that the pump can drive to, and from which atoms relax non-radiatively (without emitting a useful photon) into the upper laser level. In a three-level scheme, atoms in the ground state |1⟩ are pumped to a high-energy band |3⟩, which decays rapidly to a metastable |2⟩, and the lasing transition is |2⟩ → |1⟩. Inversion requires that more than half of the active ions be lifted out of |1⟩, because the lower laser level is the ground state itself. In a four-level scheme, an extra level is inserted between the lasing transition and the ground state. The lasing transition is now |3⟩ → |2⟩, and |2⟩ itself is a short-lived state that empties rapidly into |1⟩. Now N₂ stays essentially zero regardless of pump rate, and any positive pump rate gives N₃ > N₂. The four-level threshold pump power is hundreds of times lower than the three-level one. Maiman's first laser, the one we will meet in section 4, was three-level; almost every commercial continuous-wave laser since 1964 has been four-level.

sections.heading: 4. Maiman, 1960: the engineer beat the theorists

blocks.text: Charles Townes had built the first microwave maser (Microwave Amplification by Stimulated Emission of Radiation, a coherent oscillator using inverted molecules to amplify a single microwave line) in 1953 at Columbia, using a beam of ammonia molecules sorted by a hexapole electrostatic field to provide inversion on the 23.87 GHz inversion line of NH₃. The Schawlow-Townes paper of December 1958, in Physical Review, derived the line-width of an ideal optical extension and proposed the Fabry-Perot cavity (a pair of parallel mirrors that bounces an electromagnetic wave back and forth between them and lets a small fraction leak out through one end) as the resonator that would convert single-pass gain into oscillation. They sketched several candidate active media, including alkali vapors and rare gases. They did not pick ruby. Gordon Gould, then a graduate student at Columbia, sketched essentially the same device in a notebook in 1957 and lost decades of priority litigation over it. Bell Labs, Hughes, IBM, Westinghouse, and TRG all had teams in the field by 1959.

Theodore Maiman was a 32-year-old engineer at Hughes Research Laboratories in Malibu, working on a limited internal-research budget and outside the celebrity-physicist cluster at Columbia and Bell Labs. He chose ruby (synthetic Al₂O₃ doped with about 0.05 percent Cr³⁺ ions) as the lasing medium, against the prevailing opinion that ruby's quantum efficiency was too low. The chromium ion provides a three-level scheme: a green ⁴T₂ pump band absorbing across 400 to 600 nm, fast non-radiative decay to a metastable ²E level with a 3 ms lifetime, and a 694.3 nm radiative transition to the ⁴A₂ ground state. The gain per unit length the rod would deliver to any 694.3 nm photon passing along its axis is set by the product of the population inversion density and the stimulated-emission cross section of the transition:

blocks.caption: g(ν) is the optical gain coefficient of the medium per unit length at frequency ν, with units of inverse length. σ₂₁(ν) is the stimulated-emission cross section of the lasing transition at frequency ν, with units of area. N₂ is the number density of active ions in the upper lasing level. N₁ is the number density of active ions in the lower lasing level (the ground state ⁴A₂ for ruby). The factor N₂ − N₁ is the population inversion density. When N₂ > N₁ the gain is positive and a beam crossing a rod of length L grows by a factor exp(gL); when N₂ < N₁ the gain is negative and the beam decays. The cross section σ₂₁(ν) at line center is set by the atomic physics and, by the Einstein relations, is fixed by A₂₁ and the linewidth of the transition.

blocks.text: Maiman's apparatus was a one-centimeter ruby cylinder with parallel-polished end faces silvered for partial reflection (the resonator that Schawlow and Townes had proposed two years earlier), sitting along the axis of a helical xenon flash lamp. On 16 May 1960 he discharged a 1000 V capacitor bank through the lamp, the rod emitted a 0.5 ms pulse of red light, and the spectrum of the pulse was narrower than anything spontaneous emission could produce. Physical Review Letters rejected his manuscript as derivative of the existing maser literature. Nature published it on 6 August 1960 as a two-column note, having dropped his figure of the apparatus. Within two years every major laboratory had built a ruby laser; by 1964 Joseph Geusic at Bell Labs had a continuous-wave four-level Nd:YAG laser using Nd³⁺ ions in yttrium aluminum garnet, with a threshold pump power orders of magnitude below ruby's. The 1964 Nobel Prize in Physics went to Townes, Basov, and Prokhorov for the maser principle, not to Maiman for the device. The omission still rankles laser historians, and rankled Maiman until his death in 2007.

quiz.question: Maiman's ruby is a three-level system: ground state ⁴A₂, pumped to the ⁴T₂ band, fast non-radiative decay to a metastable ²E, then a stimulated 694.3 nm transition back to ⁴A₂. To reach inversion in such a scheme, what fraction of the chromium ions in the rod must be sitting in the metastable ²E level, and what is the practical consequence for the pump power required?
options: About 5 percent. The Boltzmann factor at room temperature determines the threshold; the pump only needs to overcome thermal occupation of the metastable level.

Greater than 50 percent. The lower laser level is the ground state itself, so N₁ starts loaded with every chromium ion in the rod, and inversion (N₂ > N₁) requires moving more than half of them up. The threshold pump power is correspondingly high. Ruby lasers operate in pulsed mode at kilowatts of flash-lamp peak input, while a four-level Nd:YAG laser of similar output reaches inversion at a few watts of continuous pumping because its lower laser level decays fast to ground and stays essentially empty.
Less than 0.01 percent. Stimulated emission only requires more atoms in the upper state than the equilibrium Boltzmann ratio, which is negligible at visible frequencies.
Exactly 25 percent in a three-level scheme and exactly 50 percent in a four-level scheme; the numbers come from the spin multiplicities of the involved states.
quiz.explanation: Option 0 confuses two unrelated thresholds: the ratio of stimulated to spontaneous emission is set by the photon occupation number, but the inversion condition is about the sign of N₂ − N₁ and has nothing to do with the temperature of any field. Option 2 mistakes the small thermal occupation of the upper state for the threshold to amplify; the question is when (N₂ − N₁) first becomes positive, not when N₂ first becomes nonzero. Option 3 invents numerology that has no physical content. The honest answer is option 1. In a three-level scheme the lower laser level is the ground state, which starts populated with every active ion (in Maiman's rod, every chromium ion). To produce inversion the pump must lift more than half of the chromium ions from ⁴A₂ to ²E, a famously demanding threshold. The required pump power scales with the volume of the active medium and with the spontaneous decay rate of the upper level (the 3 ms lifetime of ²E in ruby is what made the threshold barely reachable with a flash lamp at all). That is the practical reason ruby lasers historically ran in pulsed mode with capacitive flash-lamp discharges of hundreds of joules per pulse. The four-level scheme breaks this constraint: the lower laser level is itself short-lived and empties rapidly into the ground state, so N₂ stays essentially zero and any positive pump rate puts N_upper > N_lower. That is why every commercial continuous-wave solid-state laser since Geusic's Nd:YAG of 1964 is four-level, and why Maiman's choice of a three-level medium was technologically the harder path even though it was historically the faster.

sections.heading: 5. NGC 4258, and a Hubble constant from a natural maser

blocks.text: In 1969 a Berkeley radio-astronomy group led by Charles Townes (by then a Nobel laureate, having shared the 1964 prize for the maser principle) detected the 22.235 GHz line of water vapor in two galactic star-forming regions, Sagittarius B2 and W49, with brightness temperatures above 10¹³ K. No thermal source could produce such a line; the kinetic temperatures of the molecular clouds themselves are 100 to 300 K. The line was an astrophysical natural maser. Water molecules in dense, shocked gas around young stellar objects were being pumped into a population inversion on the 6_(1,6) → 5_(2,3) rotational transition by collisions with H₂ (and, in some sites, by infrared radiation from a nearby protostar). Stimulated emission was then amplifying the 22 GHz line by single-pass gain factors of e²⁰ or more along sight-lines through the cloud. Einstein's third rate was not a piece of laboratory engineering; it had been running quietly in the cooler regions of the interstellar medium since long before the planet on which Einstein worked existed.

In 1995 a Japanese-American collaboration led by Makoto Miyoshi at the Mitaka Observatory pointed the Very Long Baseline Array (a continent-scale radio interferometer combining ten 25-meter antennas from Hawaii to the US Virgin Islands) at the active galactic nucleus of NGC 4258, a Seyfert galaxy in Canes Venatici twenty-three million light-years away. The angular resolution at 22 GHz was 0.2 milliarcseconds, equivalent to reading a typeface from 60,000 kilometers away. The water masers in NGC 4258 resolved out as discrete sub-parsec spots lying on a thin edge-on disk in nearly perfect Keplerian rotation around something invisible at the center. The mass enclosed, read off the rotation curve maser by maser, was 3.9 × 10⁷ M_⊙ inside a radius of 0.13 parsec, far above any reasonable stellar mass density. It was the first dynamical confirmation of a supermassive black hole from outside the inner parsec of an active nucleus, and the first geometric distance to an extragalactic source that did not depend on the cosmic distance ladder.

By 2013 the same maser disk had been turned into a one-step kinematic distance. The proper motion of individual maser spots across the sky, combined with their line-of-sight velocities from the Doppler shift of the 22 GHz line, gives a distance that is read straight off the geometry of rotation: D = 7.6 ± 0.2 Mpc. This anchors an independent rung of the local distance ladder, sidestepping the Cepheid period-luminosity calibration that every other route uses. Plugged into the local Hubble flow, the NGC 4258 distance currently delivers H₀ = 72.0 ± 3.0 km/s/Mpc, where H₀ is the present-day expansion rate of the universe in km/s per Mpc. The result sits within one sigma of the cosmic-microwave-background value (67.4) and within two sigma of the local Cepheid-supernova value (73.0), squarely in the middle of the present-day Hubble tension between those two camps.

Each of those NGC 4258 maser spots, imaged to a fraction of a milliarcsecond, is a population inversion running in steady state in a parcel of warm water vapor in deep space, with a collisional pump rate from H₂ impacts that exceeds the spontaneous decay rate of the upper rotational level at 22 GHz. The same Einstein coefficient B₂₁, written down in 1917 because the books on Planck's law did not close, is now a measuring rod for the inner geometry of an active galactic nucleus 23 million light-years away. Whether the rod ultimately resolves the Hubble tension at 70 or 73 km/s/Mpc, the third rate is doing the work.

finalQuiz.question: A laser physicist and a radio astronomer both rely on stimulated emission, but their engineering constraints invert. The physicist designs every part of the device; the astronomer surveys whatever the cosmos happens to deliver. Identify what is genuinely asymmetric between the two settings, and what is the same.
options: The asymmetry is engineering versus selection, and the symmetry is the underlying rate equation. The laser physicist designs an active medium with a level structure that admits inversion (three-level for ruby, four-level for Nd:YAG), a pump source that exceeds threshold (a flash lamp, a diode array, another laser), and a Fabry-Perot cavity that recycles emitted photons through the medium for many passes. The astronomer surveys regions of the interstellar medium where all three coincide by accident: dense H₂ gas collisionally pumps the 6_(1,6) → 5_(2,3) rotational transition of H₂O above the spontaneous decay rate at 22 GHz, the natural inversion is large, and the path length through the cloud at the right viewing angle is long enough to produce e²⁰ or more single-pass gain with no cavity at all. The astronomer cannot tune any of those quantities; she selects sight-lines on the sky where the cosmos has supplied them. What is the same in both cases is the bookkeeping: the lasing rate per unit volume is (N₂ − N₁) B ρ(ν₀), with B₁₂ = B₂₁ ≡ B by Einstein's relation, and the books close (positive net stimulated emission) only when N₂ > N₁. Einstein's third rate is the same rate.

Both physicist and astronomer require a Fabry-Perot cavity. In the cosmic case the cavity is provided by the surrounding interstellar dust, which acts as a partially reflecting mirror around the maser cloud.
Both settings need the same setup; cosmic masers are engineered by stars in the same way that ruby lasers are engineered by humans, and the relevant Einstein coefficients differ only in magnitude.

The laser physicist needs a coherent pump source (a flash lamp or another laser), and the astronomer requires an incoherent thermal pump; without the thermal pump the cosmic maser would not work, and without the coherent pump the laser would not work.
finalQuiz.explanation: Option 1 invents a dust cavity that does not exist; cosmic masers operate as single-pass amplifiers, with the line-of-sight gain length doing the work that mirrors do in a Fabry-Perot. Option 2 effaces the engineering distinction by claiming both are designed. Option 3 mistakes coherent versus incoherent for the relevant axis; pump sources can be either in either setting (a flash lamp is itself incoherent, and a cosmic maser pumped by infrared radiation from a nearby protostar is being driven by a thermal field). The honest answer is option 0. The laser physicist controls three things by design: the active medium, the pump, and the resonator. The astronomer controls none of them and selects sight-lines on the sky where the cosmos has, by accident, produced them. What is shared, and what makes both projects the same physics rather than different physics, is the rate equation underneath: the net rate at which photons are added to the field is (N₂ − N₁) B ρ(ν₀), with B₁₂ = B₂₁ by the Einstein relation, and the books close only when N₂ > N₁. The third rate Einstein wrote down in 1917 to close Planck's bookkeeping is the same rate Maiman engineered in chromium ions in 1960 and the same rate that nature has been running in 22 GHz water vapor in shocked clouds for billions of years. The names of the levels differ, the values of σ₂₁ and N differ, the geometry differs; the rate is the same.
